practice: Implement linear search and analyze its complexity.
Write iterative binary search and explain each boundary update.
List three edge cases for binary search and how to handle them.
title: Algorithms Lesson 3: Searching
references: Introduction to Algorithms (Cormen et al.), Binary Search
summary: Learn when to use linear search vs binary search and how to implement each safely.
content:
# Algorithms Lesson 3: Searching

## Overview
Searching finds a target item in a collection. The best approach depends on ordering, access costs, and query frequency.

## Linear search
- Works on unsorted data
- Worst-case time is O(n)
- Simple and reliable for small collections

## Binary search
- Requires sorted data and random access
- Time complexity is O(log n)
- Must update bounds carefully to avoid infinite loops

## Correctness checklist
1. Set low = 0 and high = n - 1
2. Compute mid with integer division
3. If value < target, move low up; else move high down
4. Stop when low > high

## When to choose which
Use linear search for one-off scans or unsorted input. Use binary search when the input is sorted or can be sorted once and searched many times.
examples: Search a log list of user IDs (unsorted) for a specific ID.
Search a sorted list of product SKUs for a target.
Explain why binary search fails on unsorted input.
objectives: Differentiate between linear and binary search use cases.
Implement binary search without off-by-one errors.
Explain time complexity for both searches.
